variables.content: The computed quasiparticle effective mass m* for paramagnetic 2D-UEL exhibits a systematic dependence on the simulation-cell electron number N that is empirically well described by the form m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} for some constant β; applying this N^{-3/2} empirical scaling to finite-N VMC and DMC data (for example N = 146, 218, 302) permits controlled extrapolation to estimates of m*(∞) in the thermodynamic limit. Observationally in the reported datasets, finite-size errors of m* are smaller in VMC than in DMC and the finite-size errors grow as the density is reduced (larger r_s).

Let N denote the number of electrons in a finite periodic simulation cell used to compute quasiparticle bands by quantum Monte Carlo; for paramagnetic (ζ = 0) 2D-UEL data obtained at N = 146, 218, and 302 electrons and for densities in the metallic range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5, the finite-N dependence of the extracted quasiparticle effective mass m*(N) is empirically well described by m*(N) = m*(∞) + α·N^{-3/2} for some constant α, and extrapolation of the VMC and DMC data using this N^{-3/2} scaling yields thermodynamic-limit estimates m*(∞) that increase monotonically with r_s across the studied points, although the VMC-extrapolated and DMC-extrapolated m*(r_s) trends have different slopes.

For the two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) model consisting of N interacting electrons of charge −e and bare mass m in a finite periodic simulation cell of area A (so that the density is ρ = N/A) and with density parameter r_s = (1/(πρ))^{1/2}a_B^{-1} (using atomic units with Bohr radius a_B = 1), the finite-size dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass m*(N) obtained from quantum Monte Carlo band-derivative estimates is taken to follow the empirical form
      m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2}
      for some constant β, and this empirical scaling relation is applied to the authors' simulation data obtained at N = 146, 218, and 302 electrons to extrapolate m*(∞) for densities in the metallic range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5.
      Holzmann et al., Theory of finite size effects for electronic quantum Monte Carlo calculations of liquids and solids, Phys. Rev. B 94, 035126 (2016).

For least-squares fitting of computed quasiparticle energies ε(k) versus momentum magnitude k obtained from finite-N quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the 2D-UEL when more than twenty k values in the interval 0 < k < 1.7 k_F are sampled, the quartic polynomial form ε(k) = a_0 + a_2 k^2 + a_4 k^4 produces more stable fits (smaller residuals and more reproducible derivative at k_F across finite-N datasets) than the Padé rational form ε(k) = (a_0 + a_1 k + a_2 k^2 + a_3 k^3)/(1 + 2 a_3 k), and the quartic fits yield slightly larger values of the extracted effective mass m* = k_F · (dε/dk)^{-1}_{k_F} for the datasets considered in the study, making the quartic choice a practically preferable and systematically biasing choice for the authors' finite-N QMC datasets in the examined parameter range.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) with spin polarization ζ = 0 and for density parameters in the metallic interval 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5, applying Slater–Jastrow–backflow (SJB) trial wave functions, band fitting (quartic or Padé), and the empirical finite-size extrapolation m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} to data at N = 146, 218, and 302 yields extrapolated thermodynamic-limit values m*(∞) that form a monotonic increasing function of r_s across the studied discrete points; this monotonicity statement is conditional on those methodological choices (SJB nodal improvements, fitting form, and N^{-3/2} extrapolation) being adequate for the datasets considered.
      Fig. 7 (this paper)

Given samples U_{ab}(\omega_i) at a chosen set of real frequencies \{\omega_i\}, fit a polynomial U_{ab}(\omega)\approx\sum_{m=0}^{n}\omega^m U_{ab}^{(m)}/m! and define the electronic contribution to the effective mass tensor by \mathbf{M}=-\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{U}^{(2)} (sign convention as used in the adiabatic expansion). Provided the sampling frequencies, fit degree n, and numerical precision are adequate for the smoothness of the true U_{ab}(\omega) (so that the polynomial fit is not dominated by numerical noise or by unresolved spectral features), the fitted second-derivative coefficients U_{ab}^{(2)} yield an accurate estimate of the electronic contribution to \mathbf{M} (i.e., the extraction is robust against fitting artifacts and numerical noise and produces errors small compared to the reported mass values).

Given finite-size effective-mass data points \\(\\{(N_{i},m^{*}(N_{i}))\\}\\) at several densities and considering the family of fits \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) parametrized by \\(\\gamma\\), the proposition is: adopting a fixed exponent value \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolations across the densities considered is justified because the resulting fits produce substantially better overall goodness-of-fit statistics as measured by the reduced \\(\\chi^{2}\\) values than alternative fixed exponents over the same data sets, and therefore using \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields reliable extrapolated values \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) for these DMC data.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

In fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) the discrete imaginary-time step dτ used for walker propagation produces Trotter (time-step) errors that can materially alter computed quasiparticle energy bands and hence the derivative-based effective mass m*: in the authors' calculations for paramagnetic 2D-UEL at r_s = 5 with N = 302 electrons, the DMC band fits obtained using dτ = 0.005 a.u. and dτ = 0.2 a.u. (each run using the same walker ensemble size N_w = 2560 and the same number of propagation steps 25×10^3) exhibit markedly different band-fitting uncertainties and produce differing estimates of m*, demonstrating that insufficient time-step control can bias m* and that the common assumption that time-step errors cancel in excitation-energy differences need not hold.

When converting a fitted Fermi velocity $v_F=5.2\times10^{5}\ \mathrm{cm/s}$ into an effective mass the assumptions used are a spherical Fermi surface and that each uranium atom contributes exactly three $5f$ heavy electrons; under these assumptions $k_F$ is set by three electrons per atom and the effective mass is estimated by $m^*=\hbar k_F / v_F$, giving $m^*\approx192\,m_e$ for the quoted $v_F$.
